topic: A person finds out they were adopted through a DNA test
raw_output:
[story 1]

The printout lay crumpled on the kitchen counter, a stark, undeniable roadmap of my origins. My hands, usually steady when I was assembling IKEA furniture, trembled slightly as I reread the genetic report. It was conclusive. The results pointed to a biological link I had never considered, a family I hadn't known existed. I pushed the paper away, the sound of the plastic tearing a small, sharp noise in the quiet apartment. "No," I muttered, the word tasting like ash. I started cleaning, scrubbing the counter until the laminate gleamed, trying to scrub the information from my mind. I needed to find a way to ignore this intrusion, to treat the data like an irrelevant stain on a clean surface. The thought of confronting the people who held this secret felt like climbing a sheer, impossible wall.

[story 2]

The fluorescent lights of the clinic room seemed to hum with judgment as Dr. Evans slid the sealed envelope across the desk. I stared at the thick paper, my jaw tight. Every line of text seemed to mock my carefully constructed reality. I picked up the pen, intending to sign for the release, but my fingers hovered above the signature line. A deep, internal resistance coiled in my chest, a refusal to accept the implications. I took a long, slow breath, forcing my gaze to focus on a distant point on the wall, anything but the damning evidence in my hand. "This is a mistake," I said, my voice unnaturally level, though the tremor in my lower lip betrayed the internal battle raging beneath the surface. I needed to dismantle this finding, piece by agonizing piece.

[story 3]

I paced the length of the living room floor, the hardwood cool beneath my bare feet. The DNA results sat on the coffee table, an unmovable object demanding acknowledgment. I stopped abruptly, planting my palms against my thighs, trying to anchor myself against the sudden vertigo. Why would this matter? It was a coincidence, a statistical anomaly, nothing more. I picked up my phone, intending to search for the testing company, but the action felt forced, a desperate attempt to regain control over a situation that had suddenly spun out of my grasp. I slammed the phone down onto the cushion beside me. The air in the room felt thick, heavy with the weight of things I wasn't ready to process, and I resolved, with a grim set to my shoulders, to simply walk away from the evidence.
